message.content: {"top_matches":[{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.6},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.3},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent full contributions (always 20) match my high generosity on dictator/ultimatum tasks and high secondary conscientiousness — reliable, norm-following cooperation.","Frequent use of rewards to support others aligns with my high communion score and stated desire to help people in need.","DUCK’s strong and sustained rewarding behavior plus transition to full cooperation resembles a communal, relationship-maintaining style that fits my profile though Duck started less cooperatively early on.","SLOTH’s mostly high contributions and occasional larger reward (round 28) fit a conscientious, steady cooperator but with slightly more variation than I’d expect."],"uncertainty_notes":["Behavioral mapping is probabilistic — identical actions can reflect different motives (strategic vs. altruistic).","We observe only contributions and costly rewards; private motives, beliefs about others, and off-game traits (e.g., political views, age) are not directly observable.","High social desirability in the profile could bias my likely behavior toward more cooperative actions than truly preferred, which adds uncertainty."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.65},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.25},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent full contributions every round — matches high pro-sociality and strong conscientious norm-following (SLOTH always contributed 20).","Moderate rewarding of others (SLOTH rewarded peers repeatedly but was less prolific than DUCK) fits a person who is helpful and wants to appear fair but not highly chatty (low-to-average extraversion).","DUCK’s strong, repeated rewarding and pro-cooperation messages reflect the same high generosity seen in the subject’s dictator/trust/ultimatum behavior, so DUCK is a plausible secondary match.","GORILLA cooperated mostly but defected once, which makes them a weaker match for someone highly inclined to follow cooperative norms and social-desirability motives.","CHICKEN’s single defection and limited rewarding make them the least likely match given the subject’s consistent generosity in economic games."],"uncertainty_notes":["Cannot observe private motives — rewards could be strategic or signaling rather than genuine altruism.","Chat frequency is a noisy indicator of extraversion; the subject’s extraversion is near average, so both quieter (SLOTH) and more vocal (DUCK) behaviors remain plausible.","Small sample of players and actions across 6 rounds — one defection might reflect noise rather than stable preference."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.82},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.18}],"behavioral_basis":["SLOTH repeatedly rewarded all cooperators across multiple rounds — a costly, consistently prosocial pattern that matches the subject's high generosity in dictator/trust tasks and stated desire to help others.","The subject shows strong communal values, high secondary conscientiousness, and high social-desirability responding; SLOTH's consistent cooperative + rewarding behavior aligns with wanting to be seen as helpful and morally responsible.","GORILLA contributed fully every round without additional social signaling, which fits the subject's modest extraversion and steady cooperation when not seeking attention, making GORILLA the secondary match."],"uncertainty_notes":["Player motives are not observable — rewards could be strategic signaling rather than altruism.","Punishment/reward actions are anonymous to recipients; other unobserved factors (private messages, off-record intentions) could explain behavior.","Limited number of rounds and only four observed players constrain confidence in a definitive match."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.65},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.3},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["High and consistent contributions (20/20) and pro-cooperation chat (encouraging ‘go all in’ and explaining mutual benefit) fit my communal, conscientious, and helping-oriented tendencies.","Chicken’s blunt norm-enforcement (‘everyone contribute all their coins’) and consistent full contributions align with my preference for clear rules, need for closure, and willingness to help others.","Gorilla is a weak third: low chat and moderate contributions indicate a quieter, less engaged approach that partly matches my lower extraversion, but contribution levels are less consistently high than the top two."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only two rounds of behavior and limited message content constrain inference about motives.","Punishment/reward patterns (especially Duck’s switching from reward to punishment) introduce ambiguity about group norms and responses I might have produced.","Self-report traits (e.g., low self-monitoring but high social desirability) create mixed signals about how much I would publicly promote cooperation versus staying reserved."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.6},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.3},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["High extraversion and high self-monitoring -> DUCK’s frequent chat and norm-setting language matches an outgoing, socially attuned style.","High conscientiousness, need for cognition, and maximization -> consistent contributions (SLOTH) and strategic use of rewards (DUCK) fit a deliberative player aiming to optimize group outcomes over repeated rounds.","Low communal/empathy scores and stingy dictator/ultimatum behavior -> some selfish/strategic streak (reward/punish selectively or defect opportunistically) aligns a bit with GORILLA’s single defection and mixed rewarding.","High financial literacy and analytic orientation -> willingness to use communication and rewards instrumentally (seen in DUCK) rather than purely altruistically."],"uncertainty_notes":["Survey measures (social desirability high) and lab behavior can diverge; self-reports may overstate some traits.","Players’ private motivations aren’t observed—same outward actions (contribute + reward) could reflect genuine altruism or cold strategic calculation.","Only four players observed; CHICKEN’s low activity could also match a privacy-preferent, low-communion profile but lacked the consistent strategic signaling expected from this subject."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.60},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.30},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent full contributions across all 29 rounds match high agreeableness, high empathy, social desirability, and a communal orientation (willingness to benefit the group) — traits in the profile.","Frequent rewarding of others (especially SLOTH and DUCK) fits high extraversion, high agreeableness, and a tendency to maintain positive relationships (high communion/self-monitoring).","DUCK’s pattern of many small, repeated rewards and eventual stable full contributions fits a highly social, prosocial actor who may adapt behavior after initial rounds — matches high empathy, need for cognition (strategic updating), and sociability.","SLOTH’s near-consistent full contributions but sparser rewards align with a conscientious, cooperative person who is somewhat less socially proactive in rewarding — matches high conscientiousness but lower reward-giving compared to CHICKEN/DUCK."],"uncertainty_notes":["No chat or verbal reasoning available, so mapping from trait-profile to in-game rewarding patterns is inferential.","Profile shows some seemingly conflicting past behavior (e.g., zero dictator/trust-sender amounts) that could indicate context-dependent generosity; this makes perfect alignment uncertain.","All three candidates are cooperative to varying degrees; distinctions rely on interpreting reward frequency/intensity as social motivation, which could be driven by other factors (strategic motives, reciprocity signals)."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.75},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.25}],"behavioral_basis":["Profile shows very high agreeableness, empathy, conscientiousness and social-desirability — consistent with always contributing the maximum and frequently rewarding others (SLOTH rewarded multiple players every round).","High need for cognition, crystallized intelligence, and desire to be a good community member align with consistent cooperative play and active norm reinforcement (SLOTH’s repeated rewards; CHICKEN’s reciprocal rewards to SLOTH).","Low likelihood of final-round defection given strong communal orientation and social-desirability — so DUCK (who defected in round 6) is a poorer match. GORILLA’s passive behavior (no rewards sent) fits less well with the profile’s high extraversion and tendency to engage. "],"uncertainty_notes":["Observed interaction is small (6 rounds) and only shows rewarding (no punishments), so inferences are limited.","Other lab measures from the profile conflict somewhat (e.g., dictator and trust-sender choices were zero), which could indicate situational variation — this creates uncertainty about generosity in anonymous transfers versus socially observed public settings.","High extraversion might predict more visible chatty behavior (as DUCK showed), so there is some ambiguity between being the active rewarder (SLOTH) and the player who publicly messaged (DUCK). Given overall communal and conscientious profile, SLOTH remains the better match."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.75},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.15},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["SLOTH contributed the maximum in both rounds, repeatedly encouraged full cooperation in chat, and rewarded others in round 2 — behavior that matches a highly agreeable, empathetic, pro-social, and talkative personality (high extraversion and agreeableness).","CHICKEN advocated everyone contribute all coins and contributed the maximum both rounds, indicating strong cooperative norms and clear pro-social preferences consistent with my high agreeableness and desire to be a good, community-minded person.","GORILLA increased contribution substantially from round 1 to round 2 and was mildly communicative (OK), suggesting willingness to cooperate and respond to group norms; this fits my tendency to be cooperative and conscientious, though less clearly match than SLOTH or CHICKEN."],"uncertainty_notes":["Motives behind punishments/rewards are ambiguous (e.g., DUCK’s switch from rewarding to punishing).","Only two rounds of behavior — small sample and some actions (punishments) may reflect retaliation or signaling not long-term personality.","Anonymous/aggregate payoffs hide interpersonal dynamics; chat is limited and may not fully represent underlying preferences or demographics."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.6},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.3},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Frequent rewarding of others and high, sustained contributions after an early low—matches my high agreeableness, empathy, and prosocial tendencies combined with occasional initial caution (e.g., ‘I need the money, but they probably do too’). DUCK sends many small rewards to multiple players and quickly moves to full cooperation, consistent with generosity plus some early self‑concern.","CHICKEN contributes the maximum every round and consistently rewards cooperators—reflects my strong fairness/egalitarian instincts, generosity in dictator/ultimatum games, and high desire to be seen as kind and fair.","SLOTH is mostly fully cooperative with a few modest reductions early, and engages in reciprocal rewarding—this fits my tendency to be reliably cooperative but sometimes held back (e.g., by health/anxiety), though SLOTH is slightly less consistently generous than CHICKEN or as outwardly reciprocal as DUCK."],"uncertainty_notes":["Behavior is inferred only from contributions and reward actions; motives (risk preferences, mistakes, or experiment strategies) are unobserved.","Multiple players display similar prosocial patterns; assigning probabilities is subjective and other allocations could be plausible.","My introspective statements (health limits, occasional hesitation) informed weighting toward players who showed early caution then consistent cooperation."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.65},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.25},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent full contributions (SLOTH gave 20 every round) match high agreeableness, empathy, and pro-social economic choices (high dictator/ultimatum/trust generosity).","Sparse, short chat from SLOTH (single short message: \"exactly\") fits very low extraversion but occasional willingness to affirm group norms—consistent with self-report of being shy but willing to put self out there briefly.","Frequent rewarding by SLOTH aligns with tendency to be fair/kind (high social desirability and generosity).","DUCK’s strong pro-social pattern (advocating cooperation in chat and repeatedly rewarding everyone) also matches the profile’s high empathy and generosity, but Duck is more talkative/solicitous than the introverted profile would typically be.","GORILLA’s occasional defection (one round) but later rewards suggests a more strategic or conditional cooperator; this fits less well with the profile’s consistently cooperative and fairness-oriented preferences."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only four players and six rounds—behavior may reflect short-run strategy rather than stable personality.","Rewards and chat are public and strategic; players might reward to signal or punish rather than from pure altruism, so observed generosity could be motivated by experiment incentives.","We cannot observe off-screen motives (e.g., fatigue, misunderstanding, or errors) or whether some players are masking true preferences; matching is probabilistic, not definitive."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.75},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.2},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["SLOTH contributed the full 20 both rounds, proactively encouraged group cooperation in chat, and rewarded other players — aligning with the subject’s high agreeableness, strong generosity in economic games (dictator/ultimatum/trust), and tendency to encourage cooperation rather than punish.","CHICKEN consistently urged everyone to contribute all coins and contributed 20 in both rounds — matching the subject’s fairness orientation, high prosociality, and preference for cooperative, predictable behavior.","DUCK showed initial generosity by rewarding all players in round 1 and increased contribution in round 2, which partially matches the subject’s cooperative impulses, though Duck’s later punitive actions make them a weaker fit given the subject’s high empathy and low propensity to retaliate."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only two rounds observed; behavior could be strategic or noisy rather than trait-revealing.","Punishment/reward motives are ambiguous (retaliation, signaling, mistakes), and summaries hide who punished whom, complicating inference.","Chat messages are sparse and tone/intent can be misread; health/anxiety-related reluctance to speak may mean the subject would have said less than observed."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.7},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.2},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["CHICKEN contributes 20 every round and frequently rewards others — matches high conscientiousness, agreeableness, strong social-desirability drive, and pro-environment/public-good orientation in the profile.","SLOTH mostly contributes near-maximum with occasional dips and gives targeted rewards — consistent with a person who is generally cooperative and conscientious but values independence/strength and may sometimes adjust contributions strategically or competitively.","DUCK frequently uses rewards to reinforce cooperation (and rewards multiple peers), matching the persona's tendency to appear caring and to reciprocate, but DUCK’s very low initial contribution conflicts with the profile’s high social-desirability and consistent prosocial presentation."],"uncertainty_notes":["Motives (image management vs genuine prosociality) cannot be observed directly; high social-desirability in the profile could mean behavior in the lab was more about appearance than intrinsic preference.","Some early-round anomalies (e.g., DUCK’s round-1 contribution of 1) may reflect mistakes or noise rather than stable preferences.","Multiple players show overlapping prosocial behaviors (rewards and high contributions), so mapping is probabilistic rather than definitive."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.75},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.20},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["High, consistent prosocial rewarding by DUCK matches my strong giving in trust/ultimatum/dictator games and high agreeableness (I tend to reward cooperation and encourage group benefit).","DUCK’s chat encouraging full contribution and calling out a defector fits my high social-desirability, self-monitoring, and preference to appear caring and cooperative (I’d likely promote norms).","SLOTH also shows steady full contributions and periodic rewarding, aligning with my conscientiousness and cooperative streak, but SLOTH is less vocally normative than DUCK, so is a secondary match.","GORILLA shows some rewarding but also a round of free-riding (contributed 0 in round 3), which contrasts with my generally consistent cooperative contributions and higher average generosity, making Gorilla a weaker match."],"uncertainty_notes":["Behavior is inferred from a small number of rounds and limited actions (no private motives or full payoff context), so matches are provisional.","High social-desirability in my profile could make me more likely to signal cooperation publicly (e.g., chat and visible rewards), which may exaggerate similarity to DUCK.","Players might be strategically rewarding for reputation or retaliation; I may behave more from norms and generosity than pure strategy, which is not fully distinguishable here."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.7},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.2},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Frequent costly rewards by SLOTH match the profile's strong prosocial/generous tendencies (high ultimatum, dictator, and trust-giver scores) and high agreeableness.","The profile shows high social-desirability and desire to appear caring; SLOTH's rewarding others (even when everyone contributed) is a visible way to signal generosity.","DUCK's explicit pro-cooperation message and consistent full contributions align with the profile's cooperative, group-oriented preferences (high environmentalism, high acceptance of group norms).","GORILLA's inactivity (consistent contributions but no punish/reward, no chat) fits the profile less well but is plausible for a conscientious, non-confrontational person who cooperates without grand gestures."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only six rounds observed with limited action types — motives (signal vs genuine generosity vs strategy) are ambiguous.","Punishment/reward anonymity and lack of private reasoning limit inference about internal motives; social-desirability could drive ostensible generosity.","CHICKEN's mixed punitive behavior then defection could reflect context-specific strategy rather than stable personality, so exclusion of CHICKEN is tentative."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.75},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.25}],"behavioral_basis":["SLOTH consistently contributed the full 20 both rounds and actively encouraged group cooperation in chat ('go all in!', 'no mind games'), matching my high agreeableness, conscientiousness, and strong social-desirability to appear cooperative and fair.","SLOTH rewarded other players in round 2, consistent with my tendency to return generosity (trust/ultimatum behavior) rather than punish—I would be more likely to reinforce cooperation than to retaliate.","CHICKEN also advocated full contribution and contributed 20 both rounds, which fits my public-facing cooperative ideal and desire for fair group outcomes, though CHICKEN was less socially active than SLOTH."],"uncertainty_notes":["Some players (notably DUCK) display mixed or erratic reward/punishment patterns that complicate a clear match to my profile.","High measured overconfidence and perfect social-desirability suggest my stated preferences might emphasize cooperation more than my private incentives would in some contexts.","Observed actions are limited to two rounds with anonymous motives; private intentions and off-chat reasoning are not visible, so assignment is probabilistic."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.75},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.25}],"behavioral_basis":["GORILLA shows early-stage free-riding (0 and low contributions) and virtually no costly rewarding — matches the persona's self-interested, low-agreeableness / low-empathy signals (dictator comment: “I need the money”)","GORILLA shifts to full contributions only after cooperation becomes the norm, consistent with high self-monitoring and high secondary-conscientiousness in the persona (able to adapt to social expectations)","SLOTH is a secondary match because they contribute fairly consistently (often full) but sometimes reduce contributions early and send occasional rewards — this fits the persona's mixed signals (some cooperative behavior under social pressure but overall profit-orientation)"],"uncertainty_notes":["Inference is based only on contributions and reward actions; internal motives are not observed and could differ from the inferred personality","Players’ behavior may reflect experiment-specific strategies (e.g., conditional cooperation, learning, mistake) rather than stable traits","Some persona measures point toward both selfishness and occasional conformity (e.g., high self-monitoring and secondary conscientiousness), so SLOTH and GORILLA are both plausible and probabilities reflect that ambiguity"]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.7},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.25},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["Contributions: the user’s prior game behavior (moderate-to-fair offers in trust/ultimatum/dictator games) suggests they would contribute substantially but not necessarily perfectly every round — matches GORILLA’s generally high-but-variable contributions rather than SLOTH’s consistently-maximal contributions or DUCK’s persistent free-riding.","Punishment style: the user’s combination of high need-for-closure, high anxiety, and profit-oriented thoughts in trust games suggests they would enforce cooperation with targeted, moderate punishment rather than the extreme, repeatedly excessive punishments shown by SLOTH. GORILLA’s modest, repeated punishments of low contributors align with that.","Social preferences: the user shows mixed signals (low Big-5 agreeableness but high secondary conscientiousness and fairly generous behavior in other economic games). That profile fits a player who follows norms and penalizes defectors when it benefits group outcomes (GORILLA) rather than someone who never punishes (DUCK) or who punishes irrationally/extremely (SLOTH).","Occasional retaliation/reciprocity: GORILLA sometimes punishes SLOTH as well as DUCK, indicating conditional reciprocity and calibrated responses — consistent with the user’s moderate self-monitoring and competitive/agency-oriented tendencies."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observed players’ extreme punishment magnitudes (e.g., SLOTH’s very large punishments) may reflect data/reporting anomalies or different cost perceptions, making precise matching uncertain.","Some user measures conflict (low Big-5 conscientiousness vs. maximal secondary conscientiousness), so inferred game behavior is ambiguous between a strict norm-follower and a more self-interested actor.","We cannot see unobserved motives or private reasoning; identical contribution/punishment patterns could stem from different internal strategies, so assignment probabilities are approximate."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.8},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.15},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["DUCK encouraged full contribution publicly then defected in the final round — a classic strategic last-round free-ride. This matches the subject’s explicit ‘maximizing the profit’ mindset in the trust game, high maximization score, stated ‘‘I need the money’’ rationale in the dictator game, and stated goals of financial independence/self-reliance.","GORILLA contributed fully each round but took no punitive or reward actions. That aligns with low extraversion/low agreeableness (reluctance to engage in social enforcement), modestly prosocial economic choices (dictator/ultimatum/trust behaviour), and a tendency to cooperate without costly signaling or punishment.","SLOTH repeatedly rewarded other players (costly positive reinforcement of cooperation). While the subject shows some rule-following and high secondary conscientiousness which could motivate reinforcement of cooperation, the subject’s profit-maximizing statements, low empathy and explicit ‘‘I need the money’’ justifications make this pattern less likely than DUCK or a passive cooperator like GORILLA."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observed interaction is small (6 rounds) with most rounds showing full cooperation, limiting behavioral discriminability.","Punishment/reward motives are ambiguous (e.g., CHICKEN punished SLOTH despite full contributions), so actions may reflect idiosyncratic motives not captured in the subject profile.","Some subject measures conflict (low Big-5 conscientiousness vs. very high secondary conscientiousness; low empathy vs. moderately generous economic plays), so mapping to a single observed strategy is probabilistic rather than certain."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.65},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.25},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Mostly high contributions with a few modest early rounds (matches my pattern of imperfect self-discipline but generally prosocial behavior).","Frequent rewarding of other cooperators (consistent with high empathy, generosity in ultimatum/dictator measures, and a tendency to reinforce prosocial partners).","Shift toward stable full cooperation over time (fits low conscientiousness/inconsistent starts but strong pro-social values and desire for social respect)."],"uncertainty_notes":["Internal motives (e.g., strategic vs. genuine altruism) cannot be observed from contributions and rewards alone.","Multiple players show high cooperation and reward behavior (CHICKEN is maximally consistent; DUCK is highly prosocial in rewards but had an extreme initial defection), so assignment is probabilistic.","Behavioral signals may be influenced by factors not in the persona (e.g., beliefs about others, misclicks, or learning), adding uncertainty to the match."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.85},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["High, consistent contributions across rounds (mostly 20) align with this person’s generous offers in the ultimatum (60% of $5) and dictator (40%) games — SLOTH is a strong cooperator.","Strong, frequent, and sometimes extreme punishment by SLOTH matches the profile’s high agency (90th percentile), desire to be respected/protective, and high anxiety/neuroticism (79th percentile) that can produce over-reactive enforcement of norms.","Occasional lapses in contribution (rounds with 0 or low contributions for SLOTH) fit the profile’s very low conscientiousness (4th percentile) and stated wish to be more disciplined.","GORILLA is a plausible secondary match because of generally cooperative behavior with milder, targeted punishments — this maps to the subject’s empathy (76th percentile) and tendency to punish norm violators but in a less extreme, more measured way."],"uncertainty_notes":["Punishment magnitudes in the record are unusually large and may reflect cumulative/reporting artifacts; interpreting emotional intent from magnitude is uncertain.","Observed actions could reflect short-term strategic motives (e.g., retaliation, signaling) rather than stable personality — mapping of lab behavior to real-world traits has inherent noise.","Limited information about private thoughts or context (no chat) means motives (prosocial enforcement vs. spite) are inferred indirectly, increasing uncertainty."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.6},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.25},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["SLOTH consistently contributed the maximum (20) both rounds and actively encouraged full cooperation in chat, matching the profile's high empathy and generous tendencies (high dictator/ultimatum offers) and desire for group benefit.","DUCK showed active norm enforcement (rewarded many in R1, then punished multiple players in R2) and adjusted contributions upward in round 2 — consistent with a high-agency person who enforces fairness and reacts strongly to perceived slights (fits high anxiety/neuroticism and desire for respect).","CHICKEN advocated full contribution and contributed 20 both rounds, matching the profile's cooperative, fairness-oriented goals and the aspirational ‘be respected’ / ‘ought to be disciplined’ self-description."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only two rounds of behavior and brief chat provide limited evidence of stable personality—actions could be situational or strategic.","Punishment/reward targets are anonymous in the summary and motives for punishing/rewarding are ambiguous, so inferred norm-enforcement or retaliation motives are uncertain.","Some profile traits (low conscientiousness, low extraversion) could point to inconsistent or less vocal behavior that isn't uniquely distinguishable among observed players, increasing ambiguity in the match."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.65},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.25},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistently high contributions with a few modest early-withdrawals (SLOTH) matches the subject's strong communal values, high empathy, and tendency to be generous but keep something for self (ultimatum/dictator/trust game behavior and qualitative answers).","Heavy, consistent rewarding of cooperators (DUCK) aligns with the subject's high empathy and communion and with their stated preference to encourage sharing and avoid greed. Duck’s initial low contribution is less consistent with the subject, making Duck a secondary match.","Always contributing the maximum (CHICKEN) is consistent with high prosociality and social-desirability but somewhat extreme given the subject’s other measures (they tend to give a lot but typically keep a small share). Chicken is therefore a possible but less likely match."],"uncertainty_notes":["Inference based on behavioral summaries only; players’ motives could differ from inferred trait-driven motives.","Anonymity and dynamics over 29 rounds mean behavior could be strategic rather than purely preference-driven.","Observed patterns (e.g., Duck’s very low first-round contribution or Chicken’s perfect cooperation) could reflect experimental noise or identity-specific strategies not represented in the personality profile."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.6},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.35},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent full contributions across rounds (cooperative, prosocial) — matches high communion, high empathy, and generous choices in dictator/ultimatum/trust games.","Frequent rewarding of other players (especially SLOTH and DUCK) aligns with tendency to reinforce cooperation rather than punish — consistent with high agreeableness and very high basic empathy.","Minimal but affirmative communication (SLOTH’s brief \"exactly\") fits an introverted yet supportive style (low extraversion but high communal concern).","DUCK’s active encouragement and broad rewarding matches the user’s strong communal orientation and high generosity, though DUCK is more vocally engaged than the user’s low-extraversion profile would suggest."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only four players observed in a short 6-round game — limited behavioral breadth to fully distinguish subtle differences.","Messaging frequency and tone are noisy signals: an introvert can still post occasional pro-social messages, and reward patterns may reflect strategic motives rather than pure preference.","Unobserved private motives (e.g., desire for reputation, experiment framing) could cause deviations from baseline personality-predicted behavior."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.75},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.25}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent full contributions across rounds align with my strong prosocial giving (high dictator and ultimatum offers: 60%) and high communion/empathy (BES 4.6, communion 8).","SLOTH repeatedly paid to reward other players rather than punish, matching my tendency to encourage cooperation using positive reinforcement rather than costly punishment (high agreeableness, high empathy, low agency).","The pattern of active rewarding across multiple rounds (SLOTH) fits my likely behavior of not only contributing but also signaling approval to sustain cooperation, consistent with my trust-game and fairness-oriented responses.","CHICKEN’s occasional rewards to SLOTH indicate reciprocal pro-social behavior which is plausible for me but less consistent/active than SLOTH, making CHICKEN a secondary match.","DUCK’s late defection (contributed 0 in round 6) is inconsistent with my strong fairness and generosity, so Duck is unlikely to match my behavior."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observed interaction is short (6 rounds) and limited — motivations behind rewards are not directly visible.","Players’ private payoffs and unobserved messaging (except one chat) could change interpretation of motives.","My internal decision rules (e.g., exact threshold for rewarding vs punishing) may differ from the observed players’ strategies, so matches are probabilistic rather than definitive."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.55},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.35},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["High contributions both rounds (CHICKEN and SLOTH) align with my high generosity in dictator/ultimatum/trust games and communal values.","CHICKEN’s minimal but normative messaging (“everyone contribute all their coins”) fits my low extraversion (introverted) but strong desire to promote cooperative, prosocial behavior.","SLOTH’s consistent encouragement and rewarding of cooperators matches my aspiration to be charitable, engaged, and supportive, and my tendency to reward cooperative actions rather than punish.","GORILLA’s moderate, cautious contributions (low then mid) could reflect a careful, deliberative approach consistent with some measured aspects of my profile (moderate conscientiousness, high crystallized intelligence), making them a distant third match."],"uncertainty_notes":["Actions in the game are limited signals—motives (strategic vs. sincere) are ambiguous.","Punishment/reward behavior (especially DUCK’s switch from rewarding to punishing) is hard to interpret without context, so similar underlying personalities could behave differently.","Chat verbosity may conflict with reported low extraversion; a normally introverted player might still send encouragement in this setting, adding uncertainty to matching."]}
